It uniquely leverages Dr. Colditz's expertise in prospective cohort design and his group's recent work in spatial pathomics (e.g., lymphovascular invasion) and molecular subtypes to ground the model in robust tumor biology.

proposals.title: A Multi-omic Integration of Transcriptomic, Pathomic, and Radiomic Features to Predict Invasive Recurrence in Ductal Carcinoma In Situ
proposals.motivation: Ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) management is challenged by overtreatment, as current risk stratification tools inadequately predict which lesions will progress to invasive breast cancer. Pre-2026, integrated pathomic-radiomic models show promise, but lack the critical biological drivers of progression captured by molecular data. Incorporating genomic features from DCIS biopsies, particularly in diverse cohorts, is essential to build a biologically grounded, clinically actionable prediction model.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating transcriptomic profiles from DCIS biopsies with digitized histopathology and mammographic radiomics will significantly improve the accuracy of predicting subsequent invasive breast cancer events compared to models using clinical, radiomic, or pathomic features alone.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing RNA-sequencing data from diverse women with DCIS (as in Abstract P1-03-05) and link it to corresponding biopsy whole-slide images and pre-operative mammograms. Transcriptomic features will include gene expression signatures of proliferation, immune response, and stromal interaction. We will employ a multi-stage, late-fusion deep learning architecture: separate convolutional neural networks (e.g., ResNet50) will extract features from histology and mammography, which will be concatenated with transcriptomic features and processed by a final multilayer perceptron for risk prediction. Model performance will be validated against time-to-invasive recurrence using Cox proportional hazards models.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first multi-omic risk prediction model for DCIS that directly integrates molecular biology with clinical imaging and pathology. It will provide a validated tool to identify low-risk DCIS patients for whom active surveillance may be appropriate, potentially reducing overtreatment. The findings will also elucidate biological pathways linking DCIS morphology and imaging phenotypes to invasive progression.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has integrated two data modalities (e.g., pathomics and radiomics), this proposal uniquely incorporates sample-specific transcriptomic data directly from the DCIS lesion, moving beyond surrogate molecular markers. The approach builds directly on the team's expertise in spatial glycan analysis and heterogeneous pattern learning in breast cancer.

proposals.title: Evaluating the Generalizability of an Integrated DCIS-to-TNBC Progression Model Across Diverse Racial and Demographic Cohorts
proposals.motivation: Models predicting breast cancer progression, particularly from Ductal Carcinoma in Situ (DCIS) to aggressive subtypes like Triple-Negative Breast Cancer (TNBC), are often developed and validated in cohorts lacking racial and socioeconomic diversity. This limits their clinical utility and may exacerbate health disparities, as performance may degrade in populations with different genetic backgrounds, social determinants of health, and access to care. Ensuring equitable predictive performance is a critical, unmet need in precision oncology.
proposals.hypothesis: An integrated model combining histopathological spatial patterns (e.g., lymphovascular invasion), core fucosylated N-glycan distribution, and transcriptomic data will show significant variability in its discrimination and calibration performance when applied to cohorts of diverse racial/ethnic and demographic composition, with performance degradation most pronounced in under-represented groups.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing pre-2026 data from the researcher's prior work on DCIS RNA-sequencing, spatial N-glycans in TNBC, and heterogeneous patterns of lymphovascular invasion. We will integrate these multi-modal features using a stacked ensemble or a multi-input neural network architecture (e.g., using TensorFlow/Keras). The model, initially trained on a reference cohort, will be rigorously validated using external datasets such as the Carolina Breast Cancer Study and the Breast Cancer Surveillance Consortium, stratifying performance metrics (AUC, calibration plots, decision curve analysis) by self-reported race, ethnicity, and area-level socioeconomic indicators.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a benchmark for the fairness and generalizability of a state-of-the-art, biologically integrated progression model. It will identify specific population subgroups for whom model performance is suboptimal, guiding targeted model refinement and data collection. The findings will directly inform strategies for deploying equitable risk stratification tools in diverse clinical screening and prevention settings.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies note disparities in model performance, this proposal uniquely evaluates a biologically grounded, multi-modal model specifically designed for DCIS-to-TNBC progression, testing it across the full continuum of care from screening to outcome in pre-specified diverse cohorts.

proposals.title: Integrating Radiomic and Histopathologic Deep Learning Models to Predict Invasive Progression from Screening Mammograms and Subsequent Biopsies
proposals.motivation: Screening mammography detects ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS), but risk stratification for progression to invasive breast cancer remains imprecise, leading to potential overtreatment. Pre-2026, advanced methods in digital pathology (e.g., spatial glycan analysis, lymphovascular invasion detection) and radiomics have been developed separately, but an integrated framework for population-level screening is lacking. Applying such a framework to routine screening data could transform risk assessment at scale.
proposals.hypothesis: A multi-modal deep learning model, integrating features from screening mammograms and corresponding biopsy histopathology images, can accurately predict the short-term risk of invasive breast cancer among women diagnosed with DCIS in a general screening population.
proposals.method: We will leverage a large, linked dataset of screening mammograms and subsequent biopsy slides from diverse women. We will extend our prior integration framework, using a pre-trained convolutional neural network (e.g., ResNet50) to extract radiomic features from mammograms. For biopsy images, we will implement a multiple-instance learning model to capture heterogeneous spatial patterns of biomarkers, informed by our prior work on core fucosylated N-glycans and lymphovascular invasion. These feature sets will be fused via a late-fusion neural network to generate a composite risk score.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, scalable tool for personalized DCIS management directly from standard-of-care screening data. It will establish a proof-of-concept for population-level integration of radiology and pathology AI, moving beyond single-institution cohorts. The findings could inform clinical trials on de-escalation strategies for low-risk DCIS.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has analyzed mammograms or pathology slides in isolation, this proposal uniquely scales an integrated AI framework to a general screening population, linking pre-diagnostic imaging with post-biopsy molecular and spatial pathology patterns to predict invasive progression.

proposals.title: Predicting the Anatomic Location of Future Breast Cancer Events Using Integrated Histopathologic, Spatial, and Molecular Features
proposals.motivation: While risk models predict a woman's likelihood of developing breast cancer, they do not specify the anatomic location (e.g., quadrant, laterality) of a future event. This spatial prediction is crucial for targeted screening and prevention strategies, especially for high-risk women with a history of DCIS or TNBC. Pre-2026 advances in spatial transcriptomics and glycan imaging, as evidenced by Dr. Colditz's recent work, provide a novel opportunity to model localized risk.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrated features from a woman's initial breast tissue—including spatial molecular patterns from DCIS, core fucosylated N-glycan distributions, and histopathologic markers of invasion—contain predictive signals for the specific quadrant or laterality of a subsequent primary breast cancer.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing cohorts of women with DCIS (including RNA-seq data) and TNBC (with spatial glycan data). Using whole-slide images and spatially resolved molecular data, we will extract multi-scale features via pre-trained convolutional neural networks (e.g., ResNet50) and graph neural networks to model tissue architecture. These features will be integrated with clinical risk factors and trained using a multi-task learning framework (e.g., XGBoost or a neural network with a softmax output for quadrant classification) to predict the location of a future event, validated in held-out test sets.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first predictive model for the anatomic location of breast cancer, moving beyond aggregate risk. It will directly inform personalized, image-guided screening protocols (e.g., MRI focus) and provide biological insights into field carcinogenesis. The integrated analytical framework will set a precedent for spatial risk prediction in oncology.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel by framing cancer risk prediction as a spatial localization problem, a departure from traditional population-level models. It uniquely integrates Dr. Colditz's recent methods in spatial glycan mapping and heterogeneous pattern learning from DCIS for this new predictive task.

proposals.title: A Deep Learning Model for Dynamic Breast Cancer Risk Prediction by Integrating Serial Mammography and Clinical Risk Factors
proposals.motivation: Current breast cancer risk models are largely static, using a single time-point assessment, and do not leverage the rich longitudinal data from serial screening mammograms. This limits the ability to provide personalized, updated risk estimates that could inform tailored screening intervals and preventive strategies, particularly for women with a history of ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) who remain at elevated risk.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a deep learning model trained on sequences of mammograms, combined with clinical risk factors, can generate dynamic, temporally updated risk scores that more accurately predict the 3-year risk of invasive breast cancer or DCIS progression than static models.
proposals.method: We will develop a recurrent neural network (RNN) architecture, specifically a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network, to process sequences of mammographic images (CC and MLO views) from up to four prior screening rounds for each woman. Image features will be extracted using a pre-trained convolutional neural network (CNN) like ResNet-50. These temporal imaging features will be integrated with time-varying clinical data (e.g., BMI, hormone therapy) and static genetic risk factors in a final fully connected layer to output a time-dependent risk score. The model will be trained and validated using data from cohorts like the Joanne Knight Breast Health Cohort, with outcomes of invasive cancer or DCIS recurrence.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, clinically actionable tool for dynamic risk assessment that moves beyond single-timepoint models. It will provide a framework for integrating serial imaging into personalized screening protocols, potentially identifying women who may benefit from more frequent surveillance or risk-reducing interventions based on changing risk profiles.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has used single mammograms for risk prediction, this proposal uniquely focuses on modeling the temporal evolution of mammographic parenchymal patterns as a direct input to a dynamic risk algorithm, creating a patient-specific 'trajectory' of risk rather than a cross-sectional snapshot.

proposals.title: Distinct Risk Profiles and Molecular Drivers of Invasive versus DCIS Recurrence After Ductal Carcinoma In Situ Diagnosis
proposals.motivation: Following a DCIS diagnosis, the risk of an ipsilateral breast event (IBE) is a key determinant of treatment intensity. However, clinical risk models and research to date (pre-2026) have largely treated all IBEs—whether invasive recurrence (IBC) or DCIS recurrence—as a single, equivalent endpoint. This obscures potentially distinct biological pathways and risk factors, limiting the precision of prognostication and the ability to tailor surveillance and therapy. Differentiating these outcomes is critical, as an invasive recurrence carries a significantly higher mortality risk than a DCIS recurrence.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the risk factors and underlying molecular biology driving invasive breast cancer recurrence after DCIS are distinct from those driving a subsequent DCIS event, and that these differences can be captured by integrating clinicopathologic data with spatially-resolved molecular profiling.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing cohorts of women diagnosed with DCIS, with annotated long-term follow-up for recurrence type (IBC vs. DCIS). Using pre-treatment tissue samples, we will perform RNA-sequencing (as in P1-03-05) and spatial glycomics profiling (as in P5-01-23) to characterize the tumor microenvironment. We will employ a multi-task learning framework with competing risks architecture, specifically Fine-Gray subdistribution hazard models coupled with lasso regularization, to separately model the cumulative incidence of IBC and DCIS recurrence. Features will include clinicopathologic variables, bulk RNA-seq signatures, and spatial metrics of core fucosylated N-glycan distribution and lymphovascular patterning.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first validated, biologically-informed risk prediction models that separately estimate an individual's risk for invasive versus DCIS recurrence. It will identify specific molecular features (e.g., spatial glycan patterns, expression signatures) preferentially associated with progression to invasion. These findings will provide a framework for more personalized post-DCIS management and highlight novel targets for prevention of invasive disease.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has noted differences in outcomes, no study has systematically modeled the two recurrence types as competing risks using integrated multi-omics data from the pre-DCIS treatment state. The application of spatial glycomics and multi-task learning to this specific etiologic question is novel.

proposals.title: Prospective Multi-Center Validation of an 812-Gene Classifier for Predicting Ipsilateral Recurrence in Ductal Carcinoma In Situ
proposals.motivation: Ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) is a common diagnosis, yet its management is challenging due to the inability to accurately predict which lesions will progress to invasive breast cancer. Overtreatment remains a significant concern. A previously developed 812-gene classifier shows promise for stratifying ipsilateral recurrence risk, but its clinical utility requires validation in a prospective, real-world setting.
proposals.hypothesis: The 812-gene expression classifier will demonstrate robust and clinically significant predictive accuracy for ipsilateral recurrence (invasive or DCIS) in a large, prospective, multi-center cohort of women diagnosed with DCIS and treated with breast-conserving surgery.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, multi-center cohort study enrolling women with pure DCIS. RNA will be extracted from formalin-fixed, paraffin-embedded (FFPE) surgical specimens and sequenced. The pre-specified 812-gene classifier algorithm will be applied to generate a risk score for each patient. Patients and clinicians will be blinded to the score. The primary endpoint is time to ipsilateral recurrence. Predictive performance will be assessed using time-dependent receiver operating characteristic (ROC) analysis and Cox proportional hazards models, adjusting for established clinical-pathologic factors.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide Level 1b evidence for the clinical validity of a molecular tool for DCIS risk stratification. It will directly inform the potential for a future randomized trial to test a gene-guided de-escalation strategy, aiming to reduce overtreatment. The findings will add crucial data on the biology of DCIS progression across diverse patient populations.
proposals.novelty: While retrospective validations exist, this is a rigorous prospective validation of a specific multi-gene classifier for DCIS, designed to generate evidence suitable for clinical application. It integrates modern RNA-sequencing and bioinformatics within a traditional prospective cohort framework to address a persistent clinical dilemma.

proposals.title: Integration of a DCIS-Specific Gene Classifier with Clinical Factors to Predict Invasive Recurrence Risk: A Unified Risk Prediction Model
proposals.motivation: Ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) is a common diagnosis, yet risk stratification for subsequent invasive breast cancer remains imprecise. Current clinical models rely on traditional factors like age, grade, and size, but have limited discriminatory power. A molecular classifier, developed from RNA-sequencing data in diverse women with DCIS (as in your recent work), offers a promising tool to improve risk prediction, but its integration with established clinical models is needed for clinical translation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a unified risk prediction model, which integrates a DCIS-specific gene expression classifier with traditional clinicopathologic factors, will significantly improve the accuracy of predicting invasive recurrence risk compared to models using either component alone.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing RNA-seq data and clinical data from a multi-institutional DCIS cohort. The molecular classifier will be derived using a penalized regression approach (LASSO or Ridge) on the gene expression data to generate a continuous risk score. This score will be integrated with key clinical variables (age, tumor size, grade, margin status) using a multivariable Cox proportional hazards model. Model performance will be assessed using time-dependent AUC and net reclassification improvement (NRI) against a baseline clinical-only model.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, clinically applicable risk prediction tool that synthesizes molecular and clinical data. It will provide more precise estimates of invasive recurrence risk for women diagnosed with DCIS, which can inform personalized surveillance and treatment discussions. The model will be a critical step towards de-escalation strategies for low-risk patients and intensified management for high-risk patients.
proposals.novelty: While molecular classifiers for DCIS exist, their direct integration into a unified, interpretable clinical risk model is not yet standard. This proposal uniquely focuses on creating a single, actionable prediction instrument by combining your novel RNA-seq-based classifier with the established clinical factors used in routine practice.

proposals.title: A Pre-Excision RNA-Seq Classifier for Predicting Invasive Recurrence in Ductal Carcinoma In Situ
proposals.motivation: Ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) is a non-obligate precursor to invasive breast cancer, yet current clinical management relies heavily on surgical excision for definitive risk assessment. This leads to overtreatment for many patients. A reliable biomarker that can predict the risk of invasive recurrence from a pre-surgical core needle biopsy would enable more personalized, de-escalated treatment strategies. Such a tool is critically needed to reduce the surgical and psychological burden on women with DCIS.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a gene expression signature derived from RNA-sequencing of pre-excision DCIS core biopsy specimens can accurately predict the subsequent risk of ipsilateral invasive breast cancer recurrence.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing RNA-seq data from diverse women with DCIS (as in Abstract P1-03-05) to train a classifier. The primary outcome is time to ipsilateral invasive recurrence. We will employ a supervised machine learning approach, specifically a regularized Cox proportional hazards model (e.g., LASSO-Cox or Ridge-Cox) for feature selection and risk score generation, given its suitability for high-dimensional genomic data and time-to-event outcomes. Model performance will be rigorously validated for discrimination (time-dependent AUC) and calibration using nested cross-validation and, if possible, an external cohort.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first validated molecular tool for risk-stratifying DCIS based solely on a pre-treatment core biopsy. It directly addresses the clinical imperative to move beyond histology alone for DCIS management. The findings could form the basis for future clinical trials testing active surveillance or less aggressive local therapy for patients classified as low-risk by the signature.
proposals.novelty: While prior prognostic signatures for DCIS exist, they are typically derived from excised specimens. This proposal focuses explicitly on the pre-excision, core biopsy setting, which presents a distinct technical and biological challenge due to limited tissue and sampling heterogeneity, making direct application of existing signatures non-trivial.

proposals.title: Inferring the Molecular Landscape of the Index DCIS Lesion from Recurrent Tumor Features: An Inverse Modeling Approach
proposals.motivation: Ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) is a non-obligate precursor to invasive breast cancer, but predicting which lesions will recur or progress remains a major clinical challenge. Current risk models rely on features of the initial DCIS, yet the molecular determinants of recurrence are incompletely characterized. This study addresses a critical gap by using the recurrence as a direct readout of high-risk biology to retrospectively infer the molecular features of the original DCIS, offering a novel lens on its latent aggressive potential.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the molecular and spatial features of a recurrent invasive breast cancer contain a detectable signature that can be used to accurately reconstruct the transcriptomic and glycomic profile of the antecedent DCIS lesion. Specifically, we posit that features associated with aggressive recurrence (e.g., TNBC phenotype, lymphovascular invasion) are most informative for this inverse prediction.
proposals.method: We will leverage a unique, pre-existing cohort of women with DCIS who later developed ipsilateral recurrence, with paired RNA-sequencing data from the initial DCIS and the recurrent tumor. We will frame this as an inverse prediction problem using a multi-modal neural network architecture. The model will take recurrence tumor features (e.g., transcriptome, core fucosylated N-glycan spatial distribution from prior work, and histopathological data) as input to predict the original DCIS molecular profile. We will employ attention mechanisms and adversarial training to ensure the model captures biologically plausible, sample-specific mappings rather than population-level averages.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first computational model to infer the molecular state of a precursor lesion from its recurrence, providing a novel tool for studying cancer evolution. It will identify specific molecular pathways in DCIS that are deterministic for aggressive recurrence, potentially revealing new biomarkers for risk stratification. The methodology could be generalized to other cancer types with precursor lesions.
proposals.novelty: While prior research correlates DCIS features with future outcomes, this study uniquely reverses the temporal direction, using the recurrence as a high-fidelity filter to identify the causative features in the original DCIS. The integration of spatial glycomics and transcriptomic data within an inverse modeling framework is also novel, moving beyond associative studies to a causal inference paradigm.

proposals.title: Translating a Molecular and Spatial Risk Model into a Population-Level Decision Support Tool for Ductal Carcinoma In Situ Management
proposals.motivation: Ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) management is highly variable, ranging from active surveillance to mastectomy, due to uncertainty in predicting progression to invasive breast cancer. This leads to significant overtreatment and patient anxiety. Pre-2026 advances in RNA-sequencing and spatial glycan analysis in diverse cohorts provide an unprecedented opportunity to refine risk stratification beyond traditional clinicopathologic features.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating validated multi-omic risk signatures (e.g., from RNA-seq and core fucosylated N-glycan spatial distribution) into a scalable, clinic-ready decision-support algorithm will improve the personalization of DCIS management recommendations at a population level, reducing overtreatment without compromising oncologic safety.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing RNA-seq and spatial proteomic data from diverse DCIS cohorts to train a validated ensemble risk model (e.g., random forest or gradient boosting) predicting invasive recurrence risk. This model will be integrated into a cloud-based, HIPAA-compliant decision-support tool using a modular software architecture (e.g., FastAPI backend, React frontend). The tool will input standard clinical and pathology data, output individualized risk estimates with management options, and be piloted in 3-5 affiliated screening centers to assess integration into clinical workflows and provider acceptance.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a directly implementable tool that translates complex molecular research into actionable clinical guidance for DCIS. It will provide a framework for deploying risk models in population health strategies, aiming to standardize and de-escalate care appropriately. The pilot will generate crucial data on real-world feasibility and barriers to adoption.
proposals.novelty: This moves beyond risk model development to the pragmatic integration of spatial-omic biomarkers into a scalable clinical tool, specifically for DCIS population management. It directly addresses the implementation gap by designing for clinic workflow integration from the outset, a step not yet realized in pre-2026 research.

proposals.title: Advanced Glycation End-Products (AGEs) and RAGE Signaling in the Progression of Ductal Carcinoma In Situ to Invasive Breast Cancer: A Spatial Transcriptomic and Glycomic Analysis
proposals.motivation: Epidemiological studies pre-2026 link diets high in AGEs and metabolic conditions like diabetes to increased breast cancer risk and poorer outcomes, but the biological mechanisms remain poorly defined. This is particularly critical for understanding the progression of Ductal Carcinoma In Situ (DCIS), where identifying drivers of invasion could prevent overtreatment and improve risk stratification. The role of AGEs and their receptor RAGE in modulating the tumor microenvironment to promote aggressive phenotypes, especially in triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC), is a significant knowledge gap.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that AGE-RAGE axis activation in DCIS and invasive breast cancer, particularly TNBC, drives a pro-tumorigenic microenvironment and aggressive phenotype by inducing epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT), immune evasion, and treatment resistance pathways.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing and prospectively collected RNA-sequencing data from diverse women with DCIS (extending from Abstract P1-03-05) and matched invasive cases to analyze AGE/RAGE pathway expression. Using methods from recent spatial analyses (e.g., Abstract P5-01-23), we will perform spatial transcriptomics (10x Genomics Visium) and imaging mass cytometry on tissue microarrays to map RAGE, its ligands, and downstream effectors (NF-κB, SNAIL) relative to tumor borders and lymphovascular invasion. We will integrate this with glycomic profiling of core fucosylated N-glycans, a known modification influenced by inflammation and implicated in metastasis. Sample-specific deep learning models, akin to those used for lymphovascular invasion detection, will be trained to identify heterogeneous spatial patterns of AGE-RAGE signaling predictive of progression.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first integrated spatial map linking AGE accumulation, RAGE signaling, and specific glycan modifications to DCIS progression and TNBC aggressiveness. It will identify novel, mechanistically grounded biomarkers for risk stratification in DCIS and potential therapeutic targets (e.g., RAGE inhibition) to prevent invasion and metastasis, particularly in high-risk populations.
proposals.novelty: While AGEs are known in diabetes complications, their spatial role in breast cancer progression is unexplored. This proposal uniquely integrates spatial transcriptomics, glycomics, and AI-driven pattern analysis—methods Dr. Colditz's team has recently applied—to decipher this diet-microenvironment-cancer axis, moving beyond correlation to mechanism.

proposals.title: Spatial Mapping of Advanced Glycation End-products (AGEs) and Their Receptors in the Breast Tumor Microenvironment: Associations with DCIS Progression and TNBC Outcomes
proposals.motivation: Advanced Glycation End-products (AGEs), formed through non-enzymatic glycation, accumulate in tissues with metabolic dysfunction and aging, both key breast cancer risk factors. Pre-2026 evidence suggests systemic AGEs may promote tumorigenesis, but their specific spatial distribution and receptor (RAGE) expression within the breast tumor microenvironment (TME) remain poorly characterized, particularly in high-risk states like DCIS and aggressive subtypes like TNBC. This gap limits understanding of a potentially modifiable metabolic driver of breast cancer progression.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that high levels of AGEs and RAGE expression within the tumor epithelium and adjacent stroma are spatially heterogeneous and are associated with higher-grade DCIS, progression to invasive carcinoma, and worse clinical outcomes in TNBC.
proposals.method: We will perform multiplex immunohistochemistry (mIHC) for AGEs (e.g., CML, CEL) and RAGE on formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded tissue sections from a well-annotated cohort of DCIS and TNBC cases. Building on our group's recent work in spatial pattern analysis, we will employ a sample-specific, deep learning-based pipeline (using a convolutional neural network architecture like ResNet50 for feature extraction) to quantify biomarker intensity and density within segmented tumor and stromal compartments. Spatial heterogeneity will be assessed using metrics like Moran's I and Getis-Ord Gi*, and associations with clinical-pathologic variables will be tested using multivariable regression.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first spatially-resolved atlas of AGE-RAGE axis distribution in the breast TME, directly linking it to DCIS biology and TNBC aggressiveness. It will identify specific histological patterns (e.g., peritumoral stromal hotspots) that may serve as biomarkers for progression risk. The findings will establish a foundation for targeting AGE accumulation (e.g., via dietary or pharmacologic means) as a novel prevention and adjuvant strategy.
proposals.novelty: While systemic AGEs have been studied, this project innovates by applying high-resolution spatial analytics, akin to our prior work on glycans and lymphovascular invasion, to map this metabolic stressor directly within the tissue architecture of pre-invasive and aggressive breast cancers, a scale of analysis not previously undertaken.

proposals.title: Multi-Cancer Validation of a Spatial Architecture Gene Expression (sAGE) Signature for Prognosis in Obesity-Associated Cancers
proposals.motivation: Obesity is a well-established risk factor for multiple cancers, including postmenopausal breast, colorectal, and pancreatic cancer, contributing to worse outcomes. Recent work by our group and others has identified specific spatial gene expression patterns, like core fucosylated N-glycans, linked to aggressive breast cancer biology. It is unknown whether these spatial molecular architectures, which may reflect a systemic obesity-driven tumor microenvironment, are generalizable prognostic markers across different obesity-linked cancer types.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a Spatial Architecture Gene Expression (sAGE) signature, derived from patterns of core fucosylation and lymphovascular invasion in breast cancer, will be associated with poor prognosis in other obesity-associated cancers, specifically colorectal and pancreatic adenocarcinoma.
proposals.method: We will apply our established sample-specific, weakly-supervised deep learning pipeline (based on CLAM or similar multiple instance learning architectures) to whole-slide images (WSIs) and matched RNA-seq data from The Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA) cohorts for colorectal adenocarcinoma (COAD/READ) and pancreatic adenocarcinoma (PAAD). The model will be trained to identify heterogeneous spatial patterns predictive of outcomes, initially using the breast DCIS/TNBC-derived sAGE features for transfer learning. Associations between the learned sAGE scores and overall/disease-free survival will be tested using Cox proportional hazards models, adjusted for stage and BMI.
proposals.contribution: This work will determine if a pan-cancer, obesity-relevant spatial molecular phenotype exists, providing a mechanistic link between obesity and aggressive disease across organs. It would offer a novel biomarker for risk stratification that moves beyond organ-specific staging and could inform targeted interception strategies for high-risk patients.
proposals.novelty: While spatial transcriptomics is emerging, applying a predefined spatial architecture signature derived from breast cancer to other solid tumors to test a specific obesity biology hypothesis is novel. This moves beyond cataloging differences to testing a conserved mechanistic pathway across cancer types.

proposals.title: Circulating sAGE as a Predictive Biomarker for Primary Breast Cancer Development in High-Risk Women: A Nested Case-Control Study
proposals.motivation: Primary prevention strategies for breast cancer in high-risk women, such as those with DCIS or strong family history, are limited by the inability to identify which individuals will progress to invasive disease. Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Colditz's work on DCIS and TNBC, has linked specific N-glycan signatures like core fucosylation to aggressive tumor biology and prognosis. Translating these post-diagnosis biomarkers to predict initial cancer development could enable targeted, risk-stratified prevention.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that elevated levels of circulating sialylated and core fucosylated advanced glycation end products (sAGEs), reflecting specific N-glycosylation patterns, are detectable prior to diagnosis and predict the development of primary invasive breast cancer in high-risk women.
proposals.method: We will conduct a nested case-control study within a prospective cohort of high-risk women (e.g., with DCIS, BRCA mutations, or high-risk lesions). Using pre-diagnostic serum samples, we will quantify sAGE levels via liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS). We will apply machine learning, specifically a Random Forest classifier, to integrate sAGE levels with clinical risk factors (age, family history, breast density) to build a predictive model. Model performance will be assessed using area under the ROC curve (AUC) and validated in an independent cohort.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first evidence for sAGEs as a pre-diagnostic, circulating biomarker for primary breast cancer risk. It will generate a novel, integrated risk prediction tool that moves beyond static genetic factors to incorporate dynamic molecular signatures. This could directly inform clinical trials of primary prevention agents (e.g., anti-glycan therapies or chemoprevention) for the highest-risk subset of women.
proposals.novelty: While N-glycan alterations are known in established tumors, applying sAGE measurement to pre-diagnostic samples for primary risk prediction is novel. It shifts the focus from prognostic biomarker after diagnosis to a predictive, actionable biomarker for prevention, bridging spatial tumor biology findings to population-level screening.

proposals.title: Stromal and Immune Microenvironment Transcriptomic Signatures Predict Subsequent Breast Events in Ductal Carcinoma In Situ
proposals.motivation: Ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) is a common diagnosis, but risk stratification for subsequent breast events (SBEs), including invasive recurrence, remains imprecise. Current research, including our own RNA-seq work in diverse DCIS cohorts, has primarily focused on epithelial tumor cell biology. However, pre-2026 evidence increasingly highlights the tumor microenvironment (TME) as a critical mediator of cancer progression. A systematic, transcriptome-wide investigation of the DCIS stromal and immune compartments for SBE risk is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific transcriptomic programs within the stromal and immune microenvironment of DCIS, distinct from epithelial signals, are associated with a significantly elevated risk of subsequent breast events.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing, clinically annotated DCIS cohorts with bulk RNA-sequencing data and SBE outcomes. Using computational deconvolution methods (e.g., CIBERSORTx, ESTIMATE) and cell-type-specific gene signatures derived from pre-2026 single-cell atlases, we will quantify stromal (fibroblasts, adipocytes) and immune (T-cells, macrophages) cell abundances and activity states. We will then apply regularized Cox proportional hazards models (LASSO/Elastic Net) to identify microenvironment-derived gene expression signatures predictive of SBE risk, controlling for standard clinicopathological factors.
proposals.contribution: This work will define a novel, biologically grounded transcriptomic signature for SBE risk based on the DCIS microenvironment, moving beyond epithelial-centric models. It will provide a potential tool for refining clinical management by identifying high-risk DCIS patients who may benefit from more aggressive therapy and low-risk patients for whom de-escalation is appropriate.
proposals.novelty: While prior work links general TME features to cancer outcomes, this proposal uniquely prioritizes a comprehensive, deconvolved analysis of the non-epithelial transcriptome specifically for DCIS progression risk, inverting the traditional focus from the tumor to its surrounding stroma and immune context.

proposals.title: Validation of Ductal Carcinoma In Situ Transcriptomic Signatures for Age, Grade, Race, and Invasive Progression in a Multi-Center Cohort
proposals.motivation: Ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) is a common precursor to invasive breast cancer, but its heterogeneous biology and variable progression risk are poorly understood. Pre-2026 studies, including our own RNA-seq work in diverse women, have identified candidate differentially expressed genes (DEGs) linked to key clinical factors, but these findings require rigorous validation to translate into reliable biomarkers for risk stratification and targeted prevention.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the significant DEGs previously associated with age at diagnosis, nuclear grade, self-reported race, and subsequent invasive second breast events (SBEs) in DCIS will retain their association and predictive value when tested in a larger, independent, multi-institutional cohort.
proposals.method: We will assemble a validation cohort of 800-1000 DCIS cases with formalin-fixed, paraffin-embedded (FFPE) tissue and linked clinical outcomes from established consortia (e.g., Translational Breast Cancer Research Consortium). RNA will be extracted, and targeted expression of the pre-identified DEG panel will be quantified using the NanoString nCounter platform, chosen for its robustness with FFPE samples. Associations between gene expression signatures and clinical covariates (age, grade, race) will be tested using multivariable linear regression, while time-to-invasive SBE will be analyzed using Cox proportional hazards models, adjusting for established clinicopathologic factors.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide robust, validated transcriptomic signatures that clarify the biological drivers of DCIS heterogeneity and progression. It will directly inform the development of clinical assays to identify high-risk DCIS lesions requiring more aggressive intervention and low-risk lesions suitable for active surveillance, moving beyond morphology-based risk assessment.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have identified discovery-phase DCIS biomarkers, this proposal systematically validates a multi-faceted gene panel derived from diverse populations against hard clinical endpoints in a large, independent cohort, a critical and often-missing step in translational research.

proposals.title: A Deep Learning Framework for Integrative Risk Prediction of Subsequent Breast Events in Women with Ductal Carcinoma In Situ
proposals.motivation: Ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) is a common diagnosis, but risk stratification for subsequent breast events (SBE), including invasive recurrence, remains imprecise. Current models rely heavily on clinical-pathologic features and lack integration of high-dimensional molecular data and social determinants of health, which are known to influence outcomes. An integrative model is needed to improve personalized risk assessment and guide management decisions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multimodal deep learning model integrating RNA-sequencing data, clinical-pathologic variables, and area-level social determinants of health will significantly improve the prediction of SBE risk in women diagnosed with DCIS compared to existing clinical models.
proposals.method: We will develop a multimodal neural network using pre-2026 architectures. RNA-seq data will be processed via a 1D convolutional neural network (CNN) or transformer encoder to extract transcriptomic features. Clinical and social data (e.g., from the Area Deprivation Index) will be processed through dense layers. These feature streams will be fused using attention-based or concatenation layers. The model will be trained and validated on existing cohorts of diverse women with DCIS, using time-to-event loss functions (e.g., Cox partial likelihood) for SBE prediction.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, validated risk prediction tool for DCIS that leverages multi-scale data. It will identify key molecular and social features associated with progression risk, providing biological insights. The tool aims to aid clinical decision-making for adjuvant therapy and surveillance intensity.
proposals.novelty: While deep learning has been applied to cancer genomics, its application to DCIS risk prediction by jointly modeling transcriptomic, clinical, and social data is novel. This approach moves beyond standard regression models and single-data-type analyses to capture complex, non-linear interactions influencing outcomes.

ideas.title: Prospective, Real-Time Cohort for Adaptive Clinical Decision Support
ideas.description: This project establishes a multi-center prospective cohort of premenopausal women with serial biospecimen and imaging collection, but uniquely embeds a real-time clinical integration pipeline from inception. The computationally efficient proteomic-mammographic risk algorithm is deployed as a clinical decision support tool at the point of care for the enrolled participants, generating immediate risk assessments. This creates a continuous feedback loop where the tool's predictions are prospectively validated against subsequent clinical outcomes within the same cohort, and discrepancies or new patterns trigger targeted, adaptive re-analysis of the accumulating proteomic and imaging data. Thus, the cohort does not just validate a static model but actively fuels its evolution, accelerating the translation of discovery into a self-improving clinical instrument.

ideas.title: Globally-Informed, Locally-Adaptive Modeling of Breast Density Trajectories and Proteomic Drift
ideas.description: This research develops a novel computational framework for analyzing longitudinal breast density and proteomic data from high-risk women. The method first learns a global, consistent model of normal aging- and time-related proteomic shifts shared across the population. It then inverts the typical penalty focus to adaptively and locally model each individual's deviations from this global trajectory, treating these personalized drifts as the signal of interest. This separation allows for the precise identification of proteomic changes predictive of future density increase or cancer risk that are specific to an individual's unique biology, moving beyond population averages. The result is a dynamic, personalized risk model that updates with each new longitudinal data point.

ideas.title: Spatial Proteomic Risk Mapping in Benign Breast Disease via Deep Learning
ideas.description: This research integrates deep learning-based computer vision with multiplex proteomic imaging to analyze the spatial architecture of protein expression across the continuum from normal breast tissue to benign disease to cancer. Convolutional neural networks will be trained to automatically extract quantitative, high-dimensional spatial features—such as cellular neighborhood compositions, protein co-expression gradients, and stromal-immune interactions—from the multiplex images of patient samples. By correlating these learned spatial features with long-term clinical outcomes from a cohort including cancer-free controls and patients with benign breast disease, the aim is to identify specific spatial proteomic patterns within benign lesions that are associated with elevated risk of subsequent breast cancer development. This creates a novel diagnostic tool that provides a spatially aware, biologically interpretable risk assessment, surpassing the capabilities of either proteomic profiling or image analysis alone.

ideas.title: A Bayesian Data Fusion Framework for Multi-Omic Risk Prediction
ideas.description: This research idea adapts a solution from geophysics, where Bayesian hierarchical models fuse disparate data types (seismic, geological, GPS) into probabilistic hazard maps, to the challenge of multi-omics integration for premenopausal breast cancer risk. The analogous solution involves formally quantifying and combining uncertainties from each data stream into a unified model. The adapted idea is to build a Bayesian data fusion framework that integrates proteomic findings with existing genomic and transcriptomic profiles. The model will treat germline genomic data as a prior risk layer, update it with transcriptomic activity evidence, and further condition it on the proteomic data, which reflects the functional cellular state. The result will be a multi-omics model that outputs a coherent, probabilistic risk prediction, explicitly representing how each biological layer contributes to and refines the overall risk estimate, thereby creating a more robust and interpretable tool for risk stratification.

ideas.title: Validate proteomic model using a multi-site, standardized validation protocol
ideas.description: This idea adapts the validation approach used for climate models to the proteomic model. In climate science, models are tested for generalizability by applying them to independent historical data from multiple, distinct locations. The adapted solution is to validate the logistic regression model's predictive proteins not in one, but in several independent premenopausal cohorts sourced from different biobanks or study centers, ensuring demographic and geographic diversity. A standardized analysis protocol would be applied to each cohort to calculate consistent performance metrics (e.g., AUC, sensitivity, specificity). This multi-site validation would provide a more robust assessment of the model's real-world applicability, highlight potential biases, and strengthen the evidence for the proteins' predictive value across a broader population of premenopausal women.

ideas.title: Adapting Geophysical Stress-Response Models to Test Protein Associations in High-Density Breast Tissue
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that known protein associations hold in the biologically distinct, high-risk context of high breast density in postmenopausal women. Drawing an analogy from geophysics, where earthquake prediction in high-stress fault zones requires direct, dense instrumentation rather than models from stable crust, we adapt this solution. The adapted research idea is to conduct a longitudinal cohort study where high-density tissue in postmenopausal women is treated as a high-stress 'terrain'. Using serial, minimally invasive sampling (e.g., image-guided biopsy or lavage) and high-plex protein analysis (akin to a sensor array), we directly measure the dynamic protein interaction networks within this specific microenvironment over time, up to and including cancer diagnosis. This approach aims to discover whether cancer development in this group is driven by a unique, localized set of protein associations and interactions not seen in lower-density tissue, fundamentally testing the original assumption.

ideas.title: Causal Deconvolution of Protein, Tissue Density, and Cancer Risk Pathways
ideas.description: This research moves beyond simple independence to map the causal architecture linking proteins, tissue density phenotypes, and cancer risk. Using paired proteomic and imaging data from prospective cohorts, integrated with genetic instruments, the system applies causal inference and mediation models to quantify the direct, indirect (density-mediated), and confounded components of protein-cancer associations. The output is a prioritized set of proteins with validated direct causal effects, alongside a mechanistic map of how other proteins operate through density-modifying pathways, revealing a more complete and actionable network of risk determinants.

ideas.title: Quantifying Field Carcinogenesis by Triangulating Against Patient-Specific and Population-Level Molecular Baselines
ideas.description: This study rigorously defines the molecular state of 'adjacent normal' tissue by establishing a patient-specific, multi-tissue baseline for comparison. For each cancer patient, we perform deep molecular profiling (whole-genome sequencing, methylation, transcriptomics) on the tumor, the surgically resected adjacent tissue, and a genetically matched distal normal tissue (e.g., blood). These profiles are then analyzed against a curated, population-level atlas of age- and tissue-matched healthy controls to statistically distinguish true, recurrent pre-malignant field effects from patient-specific constitutional variation or non-cancer-related somatic mosaicism. The final output is a quantitative, multi-omic signature of field carcinogenesis, validated for its power to predict local recurrence risk.

ideas.title: Implement a federated validation network with standardized phantom calibration
ideas.description: This expanded idea adapts a solution from medical physics, where standardized physical phantoms are used to calibrate and harmonize multi-center data in radiotherapy trials. The analogous problem involved ensuring that observed outcomes across institutions reflected true biological or treatment effects, not technical variability in imaging or delivery systems. The adapted research idea proposes creating and distributing a standardized digital 'validation phantom'—a synthetic dataset with precisely known characteristics—to all external validation sites in a larger multi-center cohort. Each site runs this phantom through their local implementation of the method. The centralized analysis of the resulting outputs will quantify inter-site technical variability and biases. This calibration step allows for the correction or harmonization of data before pooling real, diverse patient datasets, thereby ensuring that the subsequent validation of the method's robustness and generalizability accurately reflects its clinical performance across different settings and populations, rather than confounding technical differences.

ideas.title: Spatially-guided, tumor-enriched phosphoproteomics to decode cancer signaling in dense breast tissue
ideas.description: This approach overcomes the fatal signal dilution problem in dense tissue by first using spatial transcriptomic profiling to map and identify micro-domains with a high probability of containing cancer cells. These precisely mapped regions are then physically isolated using laser capture microdissection, creating purified samples enriched for the tumor compartment. Subsequent high-sensitivity, targeted phosphoproteomic analysis on these enriched samples provides a direct and accurate measurement of the activated signaling pathways specifically within the cancer cells, eliminating confounding signals from the dense stromal background. This yields a robust, biologically relevant assessment of the driving oncogenic pathways for guiding targeted therapy.

ideas.title: Apply spatial transcriptomics cell-typing algorithms to spatial proteomics for automated tissue compartment annotation
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from astronomy, where algorithms automatically detect and classify cosmic structures from spatial survey data, to the problem of mapping tissue heterogeneity in spatial proteomics. In cosmology, tools like SExtractor analyze pixelated spatial data to identify and catalog galaxies, clusters, and voids. Adapted to breast cancer research, similar computational pipelines would be developed to process imaging mass cytometry or GeoMx datasets. These pipelines would automatically segment the tissue image, identify distinct cellular and histological compartments based on multiplexed protein expression patterns, and classify them (e.g., malignant epithelium, fibroblast-rich stroma, tertiary lymphoid structures). This automated, algorithm-driven compartment annotation would replace or supplement manual pathological annotation, providing a robust, quantitative, and high-throughput foundation for precisely mapping candidate protein localization within the complex architecture of breast tissue.

ideas.title: Stability-constrained, multi-cohort ensemble feature selection for reproducible proteomic signatures
ideas.description: This redesigned system integrates machine learning with a rigorous stability framework to overcome the reproducibility crisis in biomarker discovery. It employs an ensemble feature selection process, repeated across bootstrapped samples of the primary dataset and applied in parallel to multiple independent external cohorts where available. The final signature is defined not by aggregate importance scores alone, but by a consensus metric requiring features to be consistently selected across these diverse conditions. This approach prioritizes generalizable, biologically stable protein markers over those that are high-performing but dataset-specific, yielding signatures with a higher likelihood of clinical translation.

ideas.title: Applying Soil Microbiome 'Background State' Analysis to Tumor-Adjacent Tissue
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that histologically normal tissue adjacent to a tumor is molecularly normal. The analogy comes from soil science, where high-resolution sequencing revealed that apparently normal 'background' soil often carries latent signatures of degradation. Similarly, in oncology, we adapt this solution by performing deep multi-omic analysis (e.g., whole-genome sequencing, methylome, and single-cell RNA-seq) on clinically annotated 'adjacent normal' tissue samples. The goal is to systematically identify and characterize a molecular 'field effect'—such as low-allele-frequency driver mutations, aberrant methylation, or chronic inflammatory signatures—that signifies a pre-cancerous field, thereby redefining what constitutes a true normal baseline for both research and clinical decision-making.

ideas.title: Inverse analysis: Identify protective transcriptomic signatures

ideas.title: Continuous, Federated Validation for Adaptive Clinical AI Deployment
ideas.description: This research direction develops a clinical decision support pipeline designed for continuous, federated validation and deployment. The tool is deployed across multiple, diverse clinical centers in a federated learning framework, where it provides real-time predictions while simultaneously and securely aggregating local performance metrics and model updates. This creates a continuous validation cycle, allowing the algorithm to adaptively improve its robustness and generalizability using real-world data from the very cohorts it serves, without centralizing sensitive patient information. The outcome is a clinically integrated AI system that evolves and validates itself in real-time across diverse populations, ensuring sustained performance and safety.

ideas.title: Incorporate additional imaging modalities

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Niche Modeling to Map Cancer Microenvironment Niches
ideas.description: This idea proposes using an analogy from ecology to extend the sample-specific learning method to different cancers. In ecology, Species Distribution Modeling (SDM) predicts where a species lives based on environmental variables and is successfully applied across diverse ecosystems. We adapt this by treating cellular phenotypes in a tumor as 'species' and the multiplex imaging-derived tissue architecture as their 'environment.' The analogous solution involves creating a flexible framework that learns the spatial rules (the 'niche') within one cancer type. This framework can then be systematically applied to other cancers with spatial heterogeneity by identifying the analogous key microenvironmental drivers (e.g., immune exclusion, stromal co-localization) specific to each new cancer type, enabling comparative studies of tumor microenvironment organization.

ideas.title: Jointly infer latent dependency graphs and model parameters for biopsy image errors
ideas.description: This approach develops a hierarchical Bayesian model that simultaneously learns the key parameters of a diagnostic or prognostic task and the underlying error correlation structure from multiplexed biopsy image data. The model posits a latent graph where nodes are image patches or whole samples, and edges represent statistical dependencies, with connectivity informed by spatial adjacency and clinical/technical covariates. Using variational inference, the system co-infers this graph structure and the model parameters, explicitly quantifying and incorporating the non-i.i.d. errors. This results in robust uncertainty estimates and improved generalization by correctly accounting for the complex, data-derived dependencies within and between samples.

ideas.title: Functional validation of protein drivers in the molecularly-defined cancer-adjacent field
ideas.description: This research direction first uses deep multi-omics profiling (e.g., proteomics, transcriptomics, epigenomics) on patient-matched tumor and adjacent normal tissue to rigorously define the molecular 'field effect' and identify candidate proteins that are aberrantly expressed or activated in the ostensibly normal tissue. The core innovation is the immediate functional validation of these candidate proteins using in vitro 3D co-culture models or in vivo genetically engineered mouse models that recapitulate the field. By manipulating these proteins specifically within the molecularly altered field, the research will determine which mechanisms are essential for initiating or enabling cancer development from adjacent tissue, identifying novel therapeutic targets for cancer interception and prevention.

ideas.title: Dual: Study sAGE in early vs late stage BC
ideas.description: Compare the prognostic value of sAGE in stage I patients versus stage III patients at diagnosis.
ideas.sourceTitle: Abstract P2-01-01: Lifestyle Linked Biomarker is Associated with Poor Prognosis in Estrogen Receptor Negative Breast Cancer

ideas.title: Non-linear machine learning to prioritize and design functional validation of protein mechanisms in cancer
ideas.description: This research direction employs a non-linear or deep learning classifier to analyze multi-omics data (e.g., proteomics, transcriptomics) from cancer tissues, specifically to identify non-additive and complex patterns among candidate proteins associated with disease density and progression. The key innovation is using the model's learned representations—such as specific feature interactions or latent space clusters—to generate prioritized, mechanistic hypotheses about protein function and cooperation for experimental testing. These hypotheses are then functionally validated using tailored in vitro or in vivo experiments designed to test the predicted non-linear relationships (e.g., synthetic lethality, pathway crosstalk). The results from these wet-lab experiments provide direct biological feedback to iteratively refine the machine learning model, enhancing its predictive power and ensuring its outputs are causally interpretable, not just statistically associated.

ideas.title: Use deep learning on histology images for recurrence risk.

ideas.title: Cross-Modal Foundation Model with Global Consistency and Local Modality-Specific Adaptation
ideas.description: This research direction develops a novel two-stage framework for multi-modal biomedical image analysis. First, it trains a cross-modal foundation model to extract a globally consistent, integrated feature representation from diverse data types (e.g., H&E, IHC, radiology). Second, it inverts the typical penalty focus by introducing a local adaptation module that learns sample-specific, sparse gates to dynamically emphasize or suppress contributions from individual modalities based on their local predictive utility and reliability. This approach ensures generalizable knowledge transfer from the global model while providing the flexibility to handle sample-specific variations in modality quality, missingness, and relevance, leading to more accurate and robust classifiers for complex tasks like cancer subtyping.

ideas.title: Assess protein associations in blood for biomarker development
ideas.description: Investigate if the tissue-associated proteins can be detected and are informative in blood plasma or serum.

ideas.title: Integrated Computational Triage and Scalable Functional Screening for Novel Cancer Mechanism Discovery
ideas.description: This redesigned approach establishes a systematic pipeline to move from candidate proteins to validated mechanisms. It begins with a computational triage step that integrates multi-omic data to filter out likely artifacts and prioritize proteins with strong, convergent evidence for a causal role in cancer and density-related phenotypes. Prioritized candidates then enter a medium-throughput, hypothesis-driven functional screening phase using CRISPR-based perturbation in physiologically relevant 2D and 3D culture models to quantify phenotypic impact. Finally, only the top-ranked, novel effectors from this screen are advanced to definitive in vivo validation studies in appropriate animal models, ensuring that the most significant and robust mechanistic insights are rigorously confirmed.

ideas.title: Functional validation of candidate protein mechanisms using a synthetic biology chassis
ideas.description: This idea adapts a core validation strategy from synthetic biology and metabolic engineering to functionally test candidate protein mechanisms in cancer and density. In the analogous field, the function of a predicted gene is tested by expressing it in a simplified, well-understood host organism (a chassis) and observing if it confers the predicted new capability. Adapted here, top candidate proteins identified from omics studies in cancer and density will be expressed in a standardized, non-cancerous mammalian cell line chassis engineered for specific sensitization (e.g., knockout of a tumor suppressor). The adapted solution is to then conduct in vitro experiments to see if the candidate protein alone is sufficient to induce specific, measurable cancer-relevant phenotypes—such as density-independent proliferation or matrix remodeling—in this controlled environment. This provides a direct, clean functional readout of the protein's mechanistic role, separating its effects from the confounding complexity of a native tumor microenvironment.

ideas.title: Multi-Modal Spatial Biopsy Analysis with Structured Error Modeling
ideas.description: This research direction develops a unified classification model for biopsy samples that integrates multiple spatial and molecular imaging modalities while explicitly accounting for their inherent, structured error dependencies. The model will capture correlations within a modality (e.g., spatial autocorrelation in H&E image patches) and, crucially, cross-modal correlations (e.g., how local gene expression patterns correlate with specific histomorphological features). This approach provides a principled way to weigh and fuse heterogeneous data sources based on their reliability and inter-dependencies, leading to more accurate and statistically rigorous sample classifications. The resulting framework will directly quantify how uncertainty propagates through the multi-modal data fusion process, offering a significant advance over methods that simply concatenate features or assume independent errors.

ideas.title: Adapting a 'Seismic Tomography' Approach to Map Signaling Networks in Dense Breast Tissue
ideas.description: This idea adapts the geophysical method of seismic tomography to cancer signaling research. In seismology, the internal structure of the Earth is inferred by analyzing how seismic waves propagate from multiple sources. Analogously, for dense breast tissue, we propose performing high-depth, spatially mapped phosphoproteomics on a grid of biopsy samples from and around a suspicious lesion. By treating the lesion as a signaling 'source' and analyzing the phosphorylation states across this spatial array, computational models can be used to infer the origin, strength, and propagation pathways of oncogenic signaling activity through the complex tissue architecture. This creates a functional 3D 'tomographic map' of activated signaling networks, revealing the driving pathways and their spatial influence, which could predict invasion and guide targeted therapy.

ideas.title: Develop an interpretability-first, human-in-the-loop clinical integration pipeline
ideas.description: This research designs and validates a clinical decision support pipeline that prioritizes explainable AI (XAI) and human-computer collaboration. The system translates the efficient algorithm's outputs into real-time, clinically contextual explanations (e.g., highlighting key patient data factors) presented alongside predictions within the electronic health record interface. It employs an interactive loop where the clinician must acknowledge and engage with the explanation before a recommendation is finalized, ensuring the tool augments rather than replaces clinical reasoning. The validation framework rigorously assesses both clinical utility and the development of appropriate trust, measuring outcomes related to diagnostic accuracy, workflow efficiency, and user reliance.

ideas.title: Spatially-Resolved Single-Cell Proteomic-Imaging Integration for Tumor Microenvironment Deconvolution
ideas.description: This fused approach integrates single-cell proteomics via mass cytometry (CyTOF) with complementary spatial imaging modalities, such as multiplexed ion beam imaging (MIBI) or co-registered digital pathology. By applying a sample-specific classification model, the high-dimensional protein expression data from dissociated cells is anchored to their spatial coordinates and morphological features within the intact tissue. This synergy allows for the direct quantification of how specific cell states, defined by their proteome, are organized into functional niches that influence tissue density, stromal reactions, and tumor immune evasion. Consequently, it moves beyond mere cell type identification to model how spatial relationships and local protein expression gradients drive cancer phenotypes.

ideas.title: Network-Based Decoding of Tissue-Specific Disease Signals in Blood Proteomes
ideas.description: This research direction applies network medicine principles directly to blood plasma or serum proteomic data to identify and validate dysregulated protein modules that originate from specific tissues. By constructing and analyzing protein-protein interaction networks from blood proteomes, the goal is to distinguish which interconnected protein clusters show disease-associated alterations and to computationally infer their tissue of origin, linking them back to the primary site of pathology. This enables the development of multi-protein, module-based blood biomarkers that are mechanistically informative about the disease process in a specific organ, rather than just reporting the presence of individual, often non-specific, proteins. The approach synergistically addresses the challenge of biomarker specificity by leveraging network robustness to detect coherent tissue-derived signals amidst the high background noise of the blood proteome.

ideas.title: Causal Tissue-Plasma Proteome Integration with Functional Validation
ideas.description: This study performs paired deep proteomic profiling on tissue and plasma from the same individuals but adds a causal inference layer to distinguish correlative from causative biomarkers. Genetic data (pQTLs) are used in a Mendelian Randomization framework to test for evidence of a causal relationship between tissue-derived protein signals and disease, via their circulating counterparts. The most promising causal candidates are then functionally validated in engineered experimental systems to confirm the directionality and mechanism of secretion. This approach moves beyond correlation to establish mechanistic, tissue-informed liquid biomarkers with stronger biological and clinical rationale.

ideas.title: Adapting Single-Cell RNA-Seq Barcoding for Parallel Proteomic Analysis of LCM-Derived Compartments
ideas.description: This idea expands the original concept of separately analyzing epithelium and stroma by drawing an analogy from single-cell genomics. The analogous problem of analyzing many individual cells in parallel was solved by using unique molecular barcodes to tag each cell's contents before pooled processing. We adapt this solution to the proteomic analysis of tissue compartments. By immediately and uniquely barcoding the proteins from laser-capture microdissected epithelial and stromal compartments with compartment-specific chemical tags (e.g., TMT), digests from multiple patient samples can be combined. This allows for a single, multiplexed LC-MS/MS run that yields quantitative, compartment-resolved proteomic data for all samples simultaneously, scaling the original dual-analysis approach to a high-throughput, multi-sample platform while improving quantitative consistency.

ideas.title: A Depletion and Targeted Enrichment Pipeline for Detecting Ultra-Low Abundance Tissue-Associated Proteins in Blood Plasma
ideas.description: This research system directly confronts the sensitivity barrier in plasma proteomics by implementing a rigorous pre-analytical workflow to reduce dynamic range compression. It combines high-abundance protein immunodepletion with subsequent targeted enrichment of candidate tissue-derived proteins using affinity reagents, creating a 'plasma sub-proteome' enriched for signals of interest. This processed material is then analyzed via high-resolution mass spectrometry or targeted assays (e.g., Olink, SomaScan) to quantify proteins previously masked by background noise. The approach systematically tests whether this enhanced detection reveals robust, disease-relevant associations that are otherwise invisible in standard plasma proteomic profiles.

Instead of just identifying linearly correlative biomarkers, the model learns to map the intricate, high-dimensional relationship between the tissue proteomic state and its reflected signature in circulation. This allows for the discovery of composite, non-linear liquid biomarker patterns that more accurately represent specific tissue pathologies or states, significantly improving diagnostic and prognostic performance from a blood test.

ideas.title: Proactive robustness hardening via simulated multi-center stress-testing during development
ideas.description: This approach shifts validation from a final, passive step to an active, integral part of the method's development cycle. A computational framework is first used to systematically simulate the technical, clinical, and demographic heterogeneities expected across future validation cohorts, creating a suite of 'adversarial' validation scenarios. The model is then iteratively trained and redesigned to maintain performance across these simulated challenges, hardening it against specific failure modes. This ensures that by the time the method is evaluated on real, large-scale multi-center data, its architecture and parameters are already optimized for robustness, dramatically increasing the likelihood of successful generalizability.

ideas.title: Deep Spatial Point Process Networks for Lymphovascular Invasion Prediction
ideas.description: This research direction fuses spatial point process modeling with deep learning to create an end-to-end framework for predicting Lymphovascular Invasion (LVI). Instead of manually extracting summary statistics from fitted point process models, a deep neural network (e.g., a Graph Neural Network or a transformer) is designed to directly ingest the raw spatial coordinates and cell phenotypes. The network implicitly learns to characterize the complex, non-linear interaction patterns—such as attraction, repulsion, and inhibition—between tumor cells, lymphocytes, and endothelial structures. This learned spatial representation is then fused with clinical covariates in a final predictive layer, enabling superior, interpretable prediction of LVI risk from multiplex imaging data.

ideas.title: Apply Ecological Niche Modeling to Proteomic Networks for Disease Module Discovery
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from ecology's Species Distribution Modeling (SDM) to the context of network medicine. In ecology, SDMs correlate species occurrence data with environmental variables to define a species' niche and predict its distribution. Here, we treat a disease-associated protein module (identified via network medicine on proteomic data) as the 'species'. We then correlate its 'occurrence' (activity or dysregulation state) across samples with local network 'environmental variables', such as the expression levels of interacting partners, signaling pathway activity, or metabolic co-factor availability. By modeling this proteomic niche, we can move beyond static module identification to dynamically predict where and when a pathogenic module is likely to be active or stabilized within the cellular interactome, offering a more mechanistic and predictive view of disease mechanisms for biomarker and therapeutic target discovery.

ideas.title: Infer context-aware protein co-regulation networks from proteomics using multi-optic priors for robust module detection
ideas.description: This approach constructs a disease-specific protein interaction network directly from the proteomic data, rather than relying on generic databases. It uses Gaussian Graphical Models or similar techniques to estimate conditional dependencies between protein abundances, representing functional co-regulation. These data-driven networks are then refined using orthogonal biological evidence, such as shared genetic regulators from pQTL data or coordinated transcriptional changes, to prioritize biologically plausible interactions. The resulting context-aware network is used to identify dysregulated functional modules with significantly higher relevance to the specific disease condition under study.

ideas.title: Spatially-Resolved Tissue Proteomic Feature Extraction for Circulating Biomarker Discovery
ideas.description: This research direction integrates computer vision-based spatial feature extraction from multiplex tissue images with paired tissue-plasma proteomic profiling. Convolutional neural networks are used to quantify complex spatial patterns (e.g., cellular neighborhoods, protein colocalization gradients) from the tissue proteome. These high-dimensional spatial features are then statistically and computationally linked to the paired circulating proteome data from the same patients. The synergy creates a powerful discovery engine that identifies plasma biomarkers whose levels are explicitly tied to specific, quantifiable architectural or functional states of the disease tissue, thereby generating more interpretable and tissue-validated liquid biomarkers.

ideas.title: Sparse Feature Selection with Interpretable Non-linear Modeling
ideas.description: This method addresses the interpretability loss of simply replacing penalized logistic regression with a black-box model. It uses an ensemble l1-penalized logistic regression stage to identify a robust, sparse set of relevant features, preserving the scientific utility of variable selection. These selected features are then modeled using an interpretable non-linear function, such as a Generalized Additive Model (GAM) with smooth terms or a transparent neural additive model, which quantifies and visualizes non-linear effects. The final system provides both the parsimony and stability of sparse linear models and the expressive power of non-linear fits, all while remaining diagnostically actionable.
